texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane distillation  (MD)  is  a  thermal  desalination  technology that utilizes hydrophobic membrane to separates hot saline feed stream into fresh water. The separation process is driven by a vapor pressure difference created by the feed stream and permeate side, which drives evaporation of the feed stream across the membrane. Furthermore, MD can be driven  by abundantly available sources of  low-grade thermal energy [1], such as solar-thermal [2 -4] and geothermal energy [5], and capable of desalinating higher salinity feed streams, e.g. , RO brine [6 -8]. Several  MD  configurations  are  available  depending  on  how  the permeate  stream  is  collected,  such  as  direct-contact  MD  (DCMD), sweeping gas MD (SGMD), air-gap MD (AGMD), and Vacuum-based MD (VMD). While each configuration has its own unique features [9], VMD operation enables generating higher permeate fluxes with lower heat

texts.orig: A B S T R A C T
texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Vacuum-membrane  distillation  (VMD)  is  a  membrane-based  separation  process  that  utilizes  hydrophobic membrane and vacuum pressure to distillate hot saline feed stream into high purity fresh water. Despite the recent interests in the VMD, comprehensive membrane characterization and theoretical performance analysis relevant to system-level scales are limited. In this work, we present experimental and theoretical investigation of a commercial high performance PTFE membrane for the VMD application. With a careful examination of the membrane properties with a module that captures both hydrodynamic and thermal behaviors with coupled heat and mass transfer analysis, we evaluated the membrane distillation performances. Because laboratory observation does not elucidate larger scale performances, experimentally validated theoretical model is used to evaluate the performances of a PTFE membrane-based single-stage VMD system with a module length of up to 10 m at salinities of 30 and 60 g/kg. Influence of various operating conditions, such as feed temperatures, mass flow rates, and vacuum pressures, as well as temperature and concentration polarizations on the desalination performances  are  examined.  We  show  that  the  PTFE  membrane  investigated  in  this  study  can  generate  high permeate fluxes at the system-scale, a promising candidate membrane for the VMD application.

texts.orig: Many researchers have studied various types of membranes for the MD applications in general [11 -14], and more specifically for the VMD application [15 -17]. Each membrane has its own mass transfer coefficient  and this  coefficient  is  essential  to  model  performance  of  larger scale MD systems [9,18]. Depending on the mode of the MD operation, the membrane mass transfer processes are governed by or combination of Knudsen, molecular, or viscous transports, and is a function of the membrane properties, such as thickness, tortuosity, and pore size, and operating temperature and pressure [19,20]. For a chosen mode of the MD operation, however, this coefficient should stay fairly constant at typical  operating  conditions. texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, we combine a carefully designed experimental apparatus with theoretical model to characterize and predict the distillation performances of a PTFE membrane for the VMD application. A high performance PTFE membrane recently examined in a DCMD configuration  [26]  is  adopted  and  investigated  systematically  with  both distilled  water  and  artificial  seawater  at  salinity  of  60  g  per  kg  of seawater.  In  this  approach,  we  considered  both  hydrodynamic  and thermal behaviors in the feed stream as well as temperature and concentration polarizations at the membrane surface to accurately evaluate the mass transfer coefficient of the membrane. In order to validate our approach, a well-studied PVDF membrane is also investigated and the mass transfer coefficient of 1.5E-6 kg/m 2 -s -Pa is identified. This coefficient is consistent with a previous gas permeation measurement [30]. The PTFE membrane examined in this study has not been studied previously  for  the  VMD  application  and  we  show  the  membrane  mass transfer coefficient of approximately 3E-6 kg/m 2 -s -Pa. We note that this coefficient  is  ~3x  higher  than  an  overall  mass  transfer  coefficient characterized  in  Ref.  [26].  Finally,  with  the  PTFE  membrane  mass transfer coefficient, we predict the system-level VMD desalination performances  at  various  operating  conditions  and  salinities  of  typical seawater, 30 g/kg, and concentrated brine, 60 g/kg. Spatial variations in the permeate fluxes are predicted and our study indicates that, for a fixed feed inlet temperatures and vacuum pressures, maintaining the feed stream at higher flow rates is an important parameter to increase the  permeate flux.  Impact of concentration polarization at the membrane surface is also examined and reduction in the permeate flux due to the polarization effect is found to be marginal. Numerical simulations show  that  the  PTFE  membrane  can  exhibit  high  distillation  performances at the system scale, a promising candidate membrane for the VMD application.
texts.text: In this study, we combine a carefully designed experimental apparatus with theoretical model to characterize and predict the distillation performances of a PTFE membrane for the VMD application. A high performance PTFE membrane recently examined in a DCMD configuration  [26]  is  adopted  and  investigated  systematically  with  both distilled  water  and  artificial  seawater  at  salinity  of  60  g  per  kg  of seawater.  In  this  approach,  we  considered  both  hydrodynamic  and thermal behaviors in the feed stream as well as temperature and concentration polarizations at the membrane surface to accurately evaluate the mass transfer coefficient of the membrane. In order to validate our approach, a well-studied PVDF membrane is also investigated and the mass transfer coefficient of 1.5E-6 kg/m 2 -s -Pa is identified. This coefficient is consistent with a previous gas permeation measurement [30]. The PTFE membrane examined in this study has not been studied previously  for  the  VMD  application  and  we  show  the  membrane  mass transfer coefficient of approximately 3E-6 kg/m 2 -s -Pa. We note that this coefficient  is  ~3x  higher  than  an  overall  mass  transfer  coefficient characterized  in  Ref.  [26].  Finally,  with  the  PTFE  membrane  mass transfer coefficient, we predict the system-level VMD desalination performances  at  various  operating  conditions  and  salinities  of  typical seawater, 30 g/kg, and concentrated brine, 60 g/kg. Spatial variations in the permeate fluxes are predicted and our study indicates that, for a fixed feed inlet temperatures and vacuum pressures, maintaining the feed stream at higher flow rates is an important parameter to increase the  permeate flux.  Impact of concentration polarization at the membrane surface is also examined and reduction in the permeate flux due to the polarization effect is found to be marginal. Numerical simulations show  that  the  PTFE  membrane  can  exhibit  high  distillation  performances at the system scale, a promising candidate membrane for the VMD application.

texts.orig: 2. Experiment
texts.text: 2. Experiment

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  VMD  experimental  set-up  consists  of  a  flat-sheet  module,  a heater,  a  chiller,  a  glass  condenser,  a  feed  solution  tank,  a  vacuum pressure chamber (permeate tank), a vacuum pump, a pressure sensor, a gear pump, a flow meter, electrical conductivity meters (HQ40D, Hach Inc., USA.), and electronic balances. The detailed configuration of the VMD system is shown in Fig. 1. The membrane flux was measured by both electronic balances in the feed and permeate tanks. To identify the wetting, liquid penetration, and salt rejection rates, electrical conductivities  were  monitored  in  both  permeate  and  feed  tanks  during  the experiments.
texts.text: The  VMD  experimental  set-up  consists  of  a  flat-sheet  module,  a heater,  a  chiller,  a  glass  condenser,  a  feed  solution  tank,  a  vacuum pressure chamber (permeate tank), a vacuum pump, a pressure sensor, a gear pump, a flow meter, electrical conductivity meters (HQ40D, Hach Inc., USA.), and electronic balances. The detailed configuration of the VMD system is shown in Fig. 1. The membrane flux was measured by both electronic balances in the feed and permeate tanks. To identify the wetting, liquid penetration, and salt rejection rates, electrical conductivities  were  monitored  in  both  permeate  and  feed  tanks  during  the experiments.

texts.orig: Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) and polypropylene (PP) bicomposite membrane (PTF045LD0A, Pall Corp., USA) with a nominal pore size of 0.45 μ m is examined in this study. Hereafter, labeled as PTFE-0.45 membrane. The choice of PTFE-0.45 membrane is based on the recent DCMD study [26] investigated three PTFE membranes with PP  or  polyethylene  (PE)  support  layers.  In  Ref.  [26],  PTFE/PP-0.45 composite membrane have shown up to 60% higher permeate fluxes over PTFE/PE-0.45 composite membrane. In addition to the PTFE-0.45 membrane,  previously  well-studied  polyvinylidene  fluoride  (PVDF) membrane (GVHP14250, Merck Millipore Inc., USA) with a nominal pore  size  of  0.22 μ m is  also  characterized  to  validate  our  approach. Hereafter, labeled as PVDF-0.22 membrane. The detailed specifications and images of the membranes are shown in Table 1 and Supplementary Fig. 1.

The  effective membrane region is located 0.3 m away from the feed inlet. An image of the VMD system and a detailed schematic of the flat-sheet module are shown  in  Supplementary  Figs.  2  and  3.  A  mesh  spacer  (see  Supplementary Fig. 4) is used to support the membrane from the permeate side. Contact between the spacer and membrane may block the vapor transport, therefore, we estimated the membrane and spacer contact area by coating colored ink on the spacer mesh and operating under vacuum. texts.orig: The  module  is  designed  for  a  fully  developed  laminar  flow.  The initial entry length of 0.3 m ensures the feed stream is hydrodynamically fully  developed  at  the  effective  membrane  region  during  the  experiments. The inlet and outlet feed stream temperatures were monitored with thermocouples. A pressure sensor (PX409-015AUSBH, Omega engineering Inc., USA.) was mounted on the permeate side of the module to  measure  the  vacuum  pressures  controlled  by  a  vacuum  pump (PC3001, Vacuubrand GMBH, Germany).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Theoretical prediction for single-stage VMD system
texts.text: 4. Theoretical prediction for single-stage VMD system

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Using the characterized PTFE-0.45 membrane mass transfer coefficient ( B ) of 3E-6 kg m 2 s 1  Pa 1 and the validated theoretical model, we conducted system-level simulations to predict the performances of a single-stage VMD module. A standard flat plate module is adopted from Refs. [9,18] with a module dimensions of 10 m length, 0.7 m width, and 4 mm flow channel depth. In order to increase the internal heat transfer, the feed stream for simulated cases are maintained fully turbulent at mass flow rates of 2 -4 kg/s, i.e. , Re > 10 4 , similar conditions examined in Ref. [9]. Because both the hydrodynamic and thermal entry lengths for the turbulent flow is much shorter than the laminar flow, less than 10 times the hydraulic diameter [37] at Re~10 4 , we assumed the flow is both thermally and hydrodynamically fully developed at the feed inlet. Feed salinities of typical seawater, 30 g/kg, and concentrated brine, 60 g/kg, are studied with various operating conditions tabulated in Table 2. A laminar case with feed flow rate of 0.3 kg/s and Re < 2300 is also simulated for a comparison (condition 1 in Table 2).

Feed salinities of typical seawater, 30 g/kg, and concentrated brine, 60 g/kg, are studied with various operating conditions tabulated in Table 2. A laminar case with feed flow rate of 0.3 kg/s and Re < 2300 is also simulated for a comparison (condition 1 in Table 2).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Simulated  permeate  fluxes  as  a  function  of  the  module  length  at different operating conditions are shown in Fig. 4. The permeate flux reduces  along  the  module  length  as  a  result  of  decrease  in  the  feed stream bulk temperature. For the higher feed inlet temperatures, the membrane  surface  temperature  as  well  as  the  driving  force  for  the evaporation are also higher, increasing the permeate flux. Alternatively, increasing the feed flow rates increases the Reynolds number, since the Nusselt number is directly correlated to the Reynolds number, higher flow rate increases the internal heat transfer. Therefore, more heat is transferred from the bulk feed stream to the membrane surface, reducing the temperature polarization and increasing the permeate flux. Comparison to the laminar case (condition 1) clearly indicates that, in order to obtain higher permeate fluxes, it is essential to maintain the feed flow at  turbulent. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusion
texts.text: 5. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We investigated the experimental and theoretical study of the high performance PTFE-0.45 membrane for the VMD application. A theoretical model based on heat and mass transfer processes is developed to understand  and  predict  the  performances  of  the  VMD  systems,  and validated experimentally. Careful examination of the membrane properties revealed that the mass transfer coefficients of approximately 3E-6 kg m 2 s 1  Pa 1 for the PTFE-0.45 membrane and 1.5E-6 kg m 2 s 1 Pa 1 for the PVDF-0.22 membrane. System-level numerical simulations are carried out to estimate the desalination performances of the PTFE0.45 membrane at larger scales at various operating conditions. Influence of the feed mass flow rates, temperatures, and vacuum pressures in the  permeate  fluxes  are  examined,  and  impact  of  the  concentration polarization at the membrane surface is found to be minimal for the salinities between 30 and 60 g/kg. Our study indicates that the PTFE0.45  membrane  can  generate  high  permeate  fluxes  at  the  system scales under various operating conditions, a promising membrane for the VMD application.
texts.text: We investigated the experimental and theoretical study of the high performance PTFE-0.45 membrane for the VMD application. A theoretical model based on heat and mass transfer processes is developed to understand  and  predict  the  performances  of  the  VMD  systems,  and validated experimentally. Careful examination of the membrane properties revealed that the mass transfer coefficients of approximately 3E-6 kg m 2 s 1  Pa 1 for the PTFE-0.45 membrane and 1.5E-6 kg m 2 s 1 Pa 1 for the PVDF-0.22 membrane. System-level numerical simulations are carried out to estimate the desalination performances of the PTFE0.45 membrane at larger scales at various operating conditions. Influence of the feed mass flow rates, temperatures, and vacuum pressures in the  permeate  fluxes  are  examined,  and  impact  of  the  concentration polarization at the membrane surface is found to be minimal for the salinities between 30 and 60 g/kg. Our study indicates that the PTFE0.45  membrane  can  generate  high  permeate  fluxes  at  the  system scales under various operating conditions, a promising membrane for the VMD application.
